currentState.nextHonestMove: Write the theorem candidate and proof-obligation surface for the four-anchor obstruction.
currentState.routeSummary: Convert the sufficiently-large-N theorem into a complete all-N resolution without overstating what is already closed or confusing imported thresholds with repo-owned claims.

exactIntervalBookkeeping.exactMaxMeaning: Use the public raw exact max for committed raw-packet claims; use the local rollout max only for compact derivative evidence.

featureExtractors.note: Reads pack route, ticket, and atom state as the baseline theorem-search context.
Uses the canonical theorem-loop as the current claim-surface ledger.
Imports structured bridge state, packet ledgers, and search-side claim hooks.
Exposes number-theory specific finite-gap and verifier-facing metadata.

claimGenerators.note: Promotes active routes, ready atoms, and next-honest-move records into candidate claims.
Turns theorem agenda items into explicit candidate claim tickets.
Projects bridge-backed structural hooks into concrete claim candidates.
Builds obstruction-alignment and repair-class candidate claims from the live packet ledger.

claimFalsifiers.note: Rejects claims that overstate the current theorem-loop claim surface.
Rejects claims unsupported by canonical artifacts already present in the pack.
Uses bounded exact interval checks to kill false local claims quickly.
Replays current bridge packets and tracked tails against candidate claims.

verifierAdapters.note: Reads theorem-loop, route, and pack artifacts as verifier-facing evidence.
Bounded exact verifier for finite interval certification.
Supplies live search-side packet and leaderboard evidence to the claim loop.
Evaluates candidate claims against tracked-tail repair surfaces and shared-prefix packets.

candidateClaims.summary: Choose the next finite-gap closure move and keep the theorem-facing claim surface honest.
candidateClaims.why: Write the theorem candidate and proof-obligation surface for the four-anchor obstruction.

candidateClaims.summary: Explain structurally why shared-prefix representative `137720141` aligns with the first failure of tail `282`.
candidateClaims.why: If that alignment is structural rather than accidental, it turns a search coincidence into a theorem-facing obstruction class.

candidateClaims.summary: Explain why completed structured tail `332` differs from current family-aware leader `432`.
candidateClaims.why: That split tells us whether the live frontier is a finite-window artifact or a genuinely better structural continuation class.

candidateClaims.summary: Model the repaired square-modulus pool as a growing family rather than treating it as already closed.
candidateClaims.why: Recent packets introduced new square moduli, so the theorem lane should aim for controlled growth, not premature closure.

candidateClaims.summary: Characterize what the top repair cluster `432, 782, 832` shares structurally.
candidateClaims.why: If the tied tails share a common repair mechanism, that mechanism is a strong candidate for the next lemma-level abstraction.

candidateClaims.summary: Shared-prefix representative 137720141 aligns structurally with the first failure of tail 282.
candidateClaims.why: If this survives falsification, it upgrades a numerical coincidence into an obstruction-class candidate.

candidateClaims.summary: Top repair class 432, 782, 832 shares a common repair mechanism.
candidateClaims.why: A shared mechanism would compress the search frontier into a reusable lemma candidate.
